title: Approved Calculator Mastery & Test Strategy
description: Turn your NCEES-approved calculator into a COGO machine: enter and add angles in DMS, convert DMS↔decimal, and use rectangular↔polar (R▸P / P▸R) to solve inverse and forward coordinate geometry in a single keystroke chain — then layer on the pacing, guessing, and handbook-search strategy that wins the timed FS exam.
key_topics: The 2026 NCEES approved-calculator list (Casio fx-115/991, HP 33s/35s, TI-30X/36X)
DMS entry, DMS arithmetic, and DMS↔decimal-degree conversion
Rectangular↔polar (R▸P / P▸R) as the one-step inverse and forward COGO engine
Inverse: distance and azimuth between two coordinates via R▸P
Forward: coordinates of a new point from azimuth + distance via P▸R
Memory registers (STO/RCL/SUM) to chain a traverse without re-keying
Area by coordinates (shoelace / sum of cross-products) on the calculator
Horizontal-curve quickies (T, L) under time pressure
FS time management, flag-and-return, guessing, and efficient handbook search

key_formulas.name: Forward coordinates
key_formulas.formula: N₂ = N₁ + D·cos(Az);  E₂ = E₁ + D·sin(Az)

key_formulas.name: Bearing→azimuth (by quadrant)
key_formulas.formula: NE: Az = β;  SE: Az = 180 − β;  SW: Az = 180 + β;  NW: Az = 360 − β

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## Speed is a skill — and it is the cheapest points on the FS

The FS gives you **110 questions in 5 hours 20 minutes — about 2.9 minutes each**. The boundary-law, ethics, and definition items are fast if you know them, but the computational questions (Survey Computations and Applied Math, ~27–41 of the 110) are where time *bleeds*. A surveyor who fumbles a DMS entry, hunts for the polar-conversion key, or hand-cranks an inverse with atan and quadrant bookkeeping will run out of clock before running out of knowledge. A surveyor whose calculator is an extension of their hand banks those points and buys time for the harder items. **Calculator fluency is the single highest-leverage exam skill that costs nothing but practice.**

This module is not new theory — Modules 4 and 5 already taught the COGO, traverse, area, and curve formulas. Here you learn to **execute** them at speed on an **NCEES-approved calculator**, and to spend the time you save wisely with deliberate test strategy.

## The 2026 approved-calculator list

NCEES limits you to a fixed list, **re-issued every year** (same list for FS, FE, PS, PE). **No programmable, graphing, or CAS calculators.** For **2026**:

- **Casio** — all **fx-115** and **fx-991** models (the name must contain "fx-115" or "fx-991").
- **Hewlett Packard** — **HP 33s** and **HP 35s** only (no other HP).
- **Texas Instruments** — all **TI-30X** and **TI-36X** models (the name must contain "TI-30X" or "TI-36X").

**Buy and master one of two:** the **TI-36X Pro** (the most-recommended FS/FE calculator — DMS, polar↔rectangular, matrices, stats, solver, two-line MathPrint display) or the **Casio fx-991EX ClassWiz** (fast, large display, excellent DMS/stat, TABLE mode). The HP 33s/35s are **discontinued** and use RPN — great for COGO chaining if you already love RPN, but not worth learning from scratch. **Always confirm the current year's list on ncees.org before your exam**, and bring a **second approved calculator** as a backup.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## The five operations every surveyor must own

Master these and you can solve almost every FS computational item quickly:

1. **DMS entry & arithmetic** — type, add, and subtract angles in degrees-minutes-seconds.
2. **DMS ↔ decimal degrees** — convert in one keystroke, both directions.
3. **Rectangular → polar (R▸P)** — turns (ΔE, ΔN) into (distance, angle): the **inverse** in one step.
4. **Polar → rectangular (P▸R)** — turns (distance, azimuth) into (ΔE, ΔN): the **forward** in one step.
5. **Memory registers** — STO/RCL (and SUM where available) to chain a traverse without re-keying coordinates.

## DMS entry, arithmetic, and conversion

Survey angles are **degrees-minutes-seconds** (e.g., 257°44′10″). Both approved families convert and compute in DMS natively, so you never have to hand-convert.

- **TI-36X Pro:** the angle key is **[2nd][DMS]** (the `°′″` menu). Enter `257°44′10″` as `257 [2nd][DMS]°  44 [2nd][DMS]′  10 [2nd][DMS]″`. To **convert a decimal to DMS**, compute the value then `[2nd][DMS] → ▸DMS`. To go **DMS → decimal**, just enter the DMS value and the calculator already stores it as decimal internally.
- **Casio fx-991EX:** the key is **[°′″]**. Enter `257 [°′″] 44 [°′″] 10 [°′″]`. Pressing **[°′″]** again (or `SHIFT [°′″]` → `▸DMS`) toggles the **answer** between decimal degrees and D°M′S″.
- **HP-33S/35S:** store angles as **H.MMSSss** and convert with **→HR** (HMS→decimal) and **→HMS** (decimal→HMS).
- **Arithmetic in DMS:** you can add/subtract angles directly — e.g., balancing interior angles, or summing a deflection. Enter each angle in DMS and press `+`/`−`; the result displays in DMS (or convert as needed). Keep the calculator in **DEGREE (DEG) mode** so all trig uses degrees.

## Why R▸P / P▸R is the heart of COGO

A survey leg has two equivalent descriptions: **rectangular** (ΔE, ΔN) = (departure, latitude), and **polar** (distance, direction). The calculator's built-in conversions move between them **in one step**, replacing a whole chain of `√`, `atan`, and quadrant logic:

- **Inverse** (two coordinates → distance + azimuth): compute `ΔE = E₂ − E₁` and `ΔN = N₂ − N₁`, feed them to **R▸P**, and read **distance** and **azimuth** directly. The calculator's R▸P returns the angle measured from the x-axis with the correct sign — combine with the handbook quadrant constant (or use `atan2`) to land in 0–360° azimuth.
- **Forward** (point + azimuth + distance → new coordinates): feed **distance** and **azimuth** to **P▸R** and read **ΔE (departure)** and **ΔN (latitude)** directly, then add to the start point. This *is* `ΔN = D·cos(Az)`, `ΔE = D·sin(Az)` done in one operation.

**Convention used here (matches the RPLS notes):** treat **x = ΔE (Easting/departure)** and **y = ΔN (Northing/latitude)**, with the angle measured as an **azimuth from north**. Some calculators define the polar angle from the x-axis; if yours does, enter ΔN as x and ΔE as y (or swap the read-out) so the angle comes out as an azimuth. Drill *your own* unit until you know which slot is which without thinking.

## Angle modes — keep it in DEG

Set the calculator to **DEGREE (DEG)** mode and confirm the **DEG** indicator before any trig. A calculator left in **RAD** or **GRAD** is the classic silent blunder — answers will be wildly wrong with no error message. Curve, traverse, and inverse work all assume degrees.

## Memory registers — chain a traverse

Use **STO/RCL** (TI: `[sto→]`/`[rcl]`; Casio: `[STO]`/`[RCL]` with variables A,B,C,…,X,Y,M) to hold running Northing and Easting totals so you never re-key a coordinate. On the TI-30Xa the **[SUM+]** key accumulates a value into memory — the basis of the leg-by-leg traverse routine in Part B. Store a constant like the vertical-curve parabola term or a fixed radius once, then reuse it. **Every value you re-key is a chance for a blunder; store it instead.**

## Fractions, reciprocals, and other time-savers

- **Reciprocal `x⁻¹`** reads a scale denominator (e.g., `f/(H−h)` then `x⁻¹`) or converts relative precision to a 1:N ratio.
- **Fraction entry** (TI MathPrint, Casio Natural display) lets you type `(A₁+A₂)/2` as a stacked fraction, avoiding order-of-operations slips.
- **Ans** recalls the last result to chain calculations; **parentheses** protect order of operations in long expressions.
- **Stat mode** (mean, sample σ) handles repeated-measurement and error-propagation items without summing by hand.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Each survey task → the calculator operation that solves it

| Survey task | Math | Calculator operation |
|---|---|---|
| Convert a field angle to decimal | `dec = D + M/60 + S/3600` | enter in **DMS**, read decimal (toggle) |
| Convert a decimal answer to DMS | — | **▸DMS** (TI `[2nd][DMS]`, Casio `SHIFT[°′″]`) |
| Add/subtract angles | balance / deflection sums | enter each in **DMS**, `+` / `−` |
| **Inverse** (two pts → distance) | `D = hypot(ΔN, ΔE)` | **R▸P**, read r |
| **Inverse** (two pts → azimuth) | `Az = (toDeg(atan2(ΔE,ΔN)) + 360) mod 360` | **R▸P**, read θ, then quadrant constant |
| **Forward** (az+dist → coords) | `ΔN = D·cos(Az)`, `ΔE = D·sin(Az)` | **P▸R**, read ΔE and ΔN |
| Bearing → azimuth | quadrant rule (below) | mental + DMS |
| Azimuth → bearing | quadrant rule (below) | mental + DMS |
| Back azimuth | `Az_back = (Az + 180) mod 360` | `±180` |
| Area from coordinates | `A = ½·|Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)|` | sum of cross-products, ÷2, abs |
| Curve tangent | `T = R·tan(I/2)` | DEG mode, `tan(I/2)` |
| Curve length | `L = R·I·(π/180)` | `R × I × π ÷ 180` or `(I/D)·100` |

## Bearing ↔ azimuth quadrant rules (memorize)

Azimuth runs **0–360° clockwise from north**. A bearing is a **quadrant letter + acute angle β**:

| Quadrant | Bearing → Azimuth | Azimuth → Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| **NE** (Az 0–90) | `Az = β` | `N β E` |
| **SE** (Az 90–180) | `Az = 180 − β` | `S (180−Az) E` |
| **SW** (Az 180–270) | `Az = 180 + β` | `S (Az−180) W` |
| **NW** (Az 270–360) | `Az = 360 − β` | `N (360−Az) W` |

The **sign of ΔN and ΔE** fixes the quadrant on an inverse: ΔN+/ΔE+ → NE; ΔN−/ΔE+ → SE; ΔN−/ΔE− → SW; ΔN+/ΔE− → NW.

## Inverse — the azimuth, robustly

The one-line bare-helper form that always lands in 0–360°:

`Az = (toDeg(atan2(ΔE, ΔN)) + 360) − 360·floor((toDeg(atan2(ΔE, ΔN)) + 360)/360)`

Note the argument order **atan2(ΔE, ΔN)** — easting first, northing second — because azimuth is measured from north (the ΔN axis). On the calculator, R▸P does this for you; the formula is the fallback when you key it by hand.

## DMS as total seconds (handy for drills/precision)

`total seconds = D·3600 + M·60 + S`, and back: `D = floor(sec/3600)`, `M = floor((sec mod 3600)/60)`, `S = sec mod 60`.

## Combined-factor and other one-key chains

- **Grid from ground:** `grid = ground × combined factor`; **ground from grid:** `ground = grid / combined factor`. Store the combined factor, multiply/divide.
- **Scale denominator:** compute the ratio, press `x⁻¹`.
- **Relative precision:** `misclosure ÷ perimeter`, then `x⁻¹` for 1:N.

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## Keystroke notation

`[2nd]`, `[SHIFT]`, `[°′″]`, `[R▸P]`, `[P▸R]`, `[x⇄y]`, `[▸DMS]` are calculator keys. **Always confirm DEGREE (DEG) mode first.** Where the TI and Casio differ, both are shown; HP-33S/35S (RPN) is noted where the Part-B resources give it.

<svg width="460" height="300" viewBox="0 0 460 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ccc">
  <!-- axes: x = E (departure), y = N (latitude) -->
  <line x1="60" y1="250" x2="420" y2="250" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="280" x2="110" y2="30" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="425" y="254" font-size="12">+E (departure, x)</text>
  <text x="86" y="26" font-size="12">+N (latitude, y)</text>
  <!-- leg from P1 to P2 -->
  <circle cx="110" cy="250" r="4" fill="#1c4e80"/>
  <text x="82" y="266" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">P1</text>
  <circle cx="330" cy="110" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
  <text x="338" y="108" font-size="12" fill="#c0392b">P2</text>
  <line x1="110" y1="250" x2="330" y2="110" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="205" y="170" font-size="12" fill="#c0392b">D (r)</text>
  <!-- delta E and delta N legs -->
  <line x1="110" y1="250" x2="330" y2="250" stroke="#1a5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="200" y="266" font-size="12" fill="#1a5">ΔE</text>
  <line x1="330" y1="250" x2="330" y2="110" stroke="#1a5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="336" y="185" font-size="12" fill="#1a5">ΔN</text>
  <!-- azimuth arc from north -->
  <path d="M 110 200 A 50 50 0 0 1 145 165" fill="none" stroke="#7a5a2a" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="120" y="180" font-size="12" fill="#7a5a2a">Az</text>
  <text x="150" y="292" font-size="11" fill="#555">R▸P: (ΔE, ΔN) → (D, Az)   ·   P▸R: (D, Az) → (ΔE, ΔN)</text>
</svg>

---

## Example 1 — Enter and ADD two angles in DMS

Add interior angles **48°17′35″** and **63°42′50″** (e.g., checking an angle sum).

**TI-36X Pro:** `48 [2nd][DMS]° 17 [2nd][DMS]′ 35 [2nd][DMS]″  +  63 [2nd][DMS]° 42 [2nd][DMS]′ 50 [2nd][DMS]″  =` → `112.0069°`; press `[2nd][DMS] ▸DMS` → **112°00′25″**.

**Casio fx-991EX:** `48 [°′″] 17 [°′″] 35 [°′″] + 63 [°′″] 42 [°′″] 50 [°′″] =` → toggles to **112°00′25″** (press `[°′″]` to switch the answer form).

**Check:** 35″ + 50″ = 85″ = 1′25″; 17′ + 42′ + 1′ = 60′ = 1°; 48° + 63° + 1° = **112°00′25″**. ✓ Subtraction works identically with `−` (used for angle balancing).

---

## Example 2 — Convert DMS ↔ decimal degrees

Convert **257°44′10″** to decimal, then convert **330.5°** to DMS.

1. **DMS → decimal.** `dec = 257 + 44/60 + 10/3600 = 257 + 0.733333 + 0.002778 = 257.7361°`.
   - **TI-36X Pro:** enter `257 [2nd][DMS]° 44 ′ 10 ″  =` → reads **257.7361°** (already decimal internally).
   - **Casio fx-991EX:** `257 [°′″] 44 [°′″] 10 [°′″] =`, then `[°′″]` to show **257.7361°**.
2. **decimal → DMS.** `330.5°`: 0.5° × 60 = 30′ → **330°30′00″**.
   - **TI:** `330.5 [2nd][DMS] ▸DMS` → **330°30′00″**. **Casio:** `330.5 = SHIFT[°′″](▸DMS)` → **330°30′00″**. **HP-35S:** `330.5 →HMS` → 330.3000 (read as 330°30′00″).

---

## Example 3 — INVERSE between two coordinates via R▸P (distance + azimuth)

P1 (N 17,095.842, E 44,600.312) and P2 (N 16,917.920, E 43,781.054). Find the distance and azimuth P1→P2.

1. **Differences:** `ΔE = E₂ − E₁ = 43,781.054 − 44,600.312 = −819.258`; `ΔN = N₂ − N₁ = 16,917.920 − 17,095.842 = −177.922`. Both negative → **SW quadrant**.
2. **Distance:** `D = hypot(ΔN, ΔE) = √(177.922² + 819.258²) = √(31,656 + 671,184) = √702,840 = 838.36 ft`.
3. **R▸P keystrokes (TI-36X Pro):** `[2nd][R▸P]` with `(ΔE, ΔN)`: enter `−819.258 [,] −177.922 [)] =`; the `r` field reads **838.36** (distance) and the `θ` field reads the angle. Press `[x⇄y]` to read θ.
   - **Casio fx-991EX:** `[SHIFT][+](Pol)`  `Pol(−819.258 , −177.922) =` → returns `r = 838.36`, `θ`. (Casio Pol takes (x, y); here x = ΔE, y = ΔN.)
   - **TI-30Xa (Part-B method):** enter `ΔE [2nd][x⇄y] ΔN [2nd][R▸P]` → DIST; `[2nd][x⇄y]` → angle; `[2nd][▸DMS]` for DMS.
4. **Azimuth:** the bare-helper form `Az = (toDeg(atan2(ΔE, ΔN)) + 360) mod 360 = (toDeg(atan2(−819.258, −177.922)) + 360) mod 360`. atan2(−819.258, −177.922) → −102.256° → +360 = 257.744° → **Az = 257°44′37″** (SW: 180° + bearing 77°44′37″). ✓ matches the Part-B worked sample (~257°44′).

**One inverse = one R▸P.** No separate √, no separate atan, no quadrant table if you trust atan2.

---

## Example 4 — Coordinates of a point from azimuth + distance via P▸R

From P1 (N 5,000.00, E 5,000.00), go **azimuth 330°30′00″**, **distance 200.00 ft**. Find the new point.

1. **Latitude/Departure:** `ΔN = D·cos(Az) = 200·cos(330.5°) = 200·0.87036 = +174.07`; `ΔE = D·sin(Az) = 200·sin(330.5°) = 200·(−0.49242) = −98.48` (NW: ΔN+, ΔE−). ✓ matches Part-B sample.
2. **P▸R keystrokes (TI-36X Pro):** `[2nd][P▸R]`  `P▸R(200 , 330.5) =` → returns the two rectangular components; one is ΔE = **−98.48**, press `[x⇄y]` for ΔN = **+174.07**.
   - **Casio fx-991EX:** `[SHIFT][−](Rec)`  `Rec(200 , 330.5) =` → `x = +174.07, y = −98.48` (Casio Rec returns (x along the polar axis); map x→ΔN, y→ΔE under the north-azimuth convention — confirm on your unit).
   - **TI-30Xa (Part-B method):** `200 [2nd][x⇄y] 330.5 [2nd][P▸R]` → DEP (ΔE); `[2nd][x⇄y]` → LAT (ΔN).
3. **New coordinates:** `N₂ = 5,000.00 + 174.07 = 5,174.07`; `E₂ = 5,000.00 − 98.48 = 4,901.52`.

**P2 ≈ (N 5,174.07, E 4,901.52).** One P▸R replaces two trig calls.

---

## Example 5 — Bearing ↔ azimuth

(a) Convert bearing **S 36°15′ W** to azimuth. SW quadrant → `Az = 180° + β = 180° + 36°15′ =` **216°15′00″**.

(b) Convert azimuth **295°40′** to a bearing. NW quadrant (270–360) → `bearing = N (360° − Az) W = N (360° − 295°40′) W = N 64°20′ W`.

**Keystrokes:** do the `180 +`, `360 −` in **DMS**: e.g., TI `180 [2nd][DMS]° + 36 ° 15 ′ =` → **216°15′00″**; Casio `360 [°′″] − 295 [°′″] 40 [°′″] =` → **64°20′00″**. **Back azimuth** is just `Az ± 180`: 216°15′ → 36°15′; 64°20′ → 244°20′.

---

## Example 6 — AREA by coordinates (sum of cross-products)

Four corners in order — A(N 1,000, E 1,000), B(N 1,000, E 1,400), C(N 1,300, E 1,400), D(N 1,300, E 1,000) — using **X = E, Y = N**.

1. **Cross-products** `Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)`:
   - A→B: 1000·1000 − 1400·1000 = −400,000
   - B→C: 1400·1300 − 1400·1000 = +420,000
   - C→D: 1400·1300 − 1000·1300 = +520,000
   - D→A: 1000·1000 − 1000·1300 = −300,000
   - Σ = +240,000
2. **Area:** `A = ½·|240,000| = 120,000 ft²` → ÷ 43,560 = **2.755 acres** (a 400 × 300 rectangle ✓).

**Keystrokes:** chain it on one line with parentheses, or store each `Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ` with `[SUM+]`/`+` into memory, then `[RCL] ÷ 2 [x⇄y]`. On the **HP-33S/35S** the stored **AREA** routine accepts sequential N,E and returns acreage directly. **Tip:** for a quick rectangle, don't run a full routine — just multiply the sides.

---

## Example 7 — Horizontal-curve quickie (T and L)

R = 1,145.92 ft, deflection I = 24°00′00″. Find T and L fast.

1. **Tangent:** `T = R·tan(I/2) = 1145.92·tan(12°) = 1145.92·0.212557 = 243.57 ft`.
2. **Length:** `L = R·I·(π/180) = 1145.92·(24·π/180) = 1145.92·0.418879 = 480.00 ft` (or `(I/D)·100` with D = 5729.58/R = 5.000° → (24/5)·100 = 480.00 ✓).

**Keystrokes (TI-36X Pro, DEG):** `1145.92 × tan( 24 ÷ 2 ) =` → 243.57; `1145.92 × 24 × π ÷ 180 =` → 480.00. **Casio fx-991EX:** identical. **Time-saver:** when D is given, `(I/D)·100` skips π entirely. Type `I/2` directly into `tan(` — don't compute I/2 separately.

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## NCEES calculator rules (don't get turned away at the door)

- Your calculator **must be on the current approved list** (2026: Casio fx-115/fx-991, HP 33s/35s, TI-30X/36X). **No programmable, graphing, or CAS** models. The proctor checks it.
- **Clear all memory** before the exam if your model can store programs/notes — NCEES expects a cleared calculator. Stored COGO programs from the HP program books are **not** allowed on the actual exam (they were study/field references, not test tools).
- Bring a **second approved calculator** and **fresh batteries** (or solar + light). A dead calculator mid-exam is catastrophic and avoidable.
- **Confirm the list on ncees.org for your exam year** — it is re-issued annually and a model can drop off.

## Calculator discipline that prevents blunders

- **DEG mode, always** — verify the DEG indicator before any trig. RAD/GRAD is the silent killer.
- **Store, don't re-key.** Use STO/RCL for running N/E totals and reused constants; every re-typed number is a blunder risk.
- **One inverse, one R▸P; one forward, one P▸R.** Don't hand-crank √ + atan + quadrant — let the conversion do it, and trust atan2 for the quadrant.
- **Sanity-check the quadrant** from the signs of ΔN, ΔE (NE/SE/SW/NW). If a point can't be SW of a known point, an azimuth near 200° is wrong.
- **Round only at the end.** Carry full precision; format with ▸DMS or fixed decimals last.
- **Use the simplest method.** Don't run a traverse routine to area a rectangle (Part D guidance) — multiply the sides.

## Time management & test strategy (from the Part-D strategies)

- **Pace ≈ 2.9 min/question** (110 in 5 h 20 min). Keep momentum, not raw speed.
- **7-step flow:** (1) preview the exam, (2) answer the **easiest first**, (3) **flag and skip** hard ones, (4) calculate, (5) **eliminate** wrong answers, (6) **educated guess**, (7) use leftover time to **review**.
- **Bank the fast points first:** boundary-law, ethics, and definition items are quick if known — don't let a 6-minute COGO problem early in the exam starve them of time.
- **Read the whole problem before keying anything** — don't enter every given coordinate until you know which corners/lines the question actually needs (Part-D calculation discipline). Solve each inverse/value **once** and store it.
- **Make a margin sketch** (vicinity sketch) for COGO/traverse/curve items — it prevents quadrant and PC/PT stationing errors.

## Guessing & flag-and-return

- **There is no penalty for wrong answers** — **never leave a question blank.** Answer every one before time expires.
- **Eliminate with strike-throughs**, then guess among what's left; watch for **distracters** that are too broad/narrow or off by a unit (ft vs. m, 27 vs. 9, 5729.58 vs. 5730).
- **Clues hide in other questions' preambles** — a definition or value you need may appear elsewhere.
- **Flag-and-return:** mark long multi-step items, move on, and come back with the time you banked from the quick ones. **Trust your gut** on split decisions; change an answer only with a concrete reason.

## Using the electronic FS Reference Handbook efficiently

- The handbook (**v2.5**) and the standards PDFs are searchable and open the **entire exam** — but the handbook is **big**. **Know where each formula lives** (DMS/trig, Inverse & quadrant constant, Horizontal/Vertical curves, Area formulas, Earthwork, Photogrammetry, Probability & statistics) so you **confirm**, not hunt.
- **Practice with the actual PDF from MyNCEES** before exam day; learn the search terms that jump to each section.
- The handbook gives the **inverse quadrant constant C** (0/180/180/360) and the curve formulas verbatim — pair them with your R▸P/P▸R muscle memory so the calculator does the arithmetic and the handbook only confirms the form.

## Common traps

- Calculator in **RAD** instead of DEG. · Mixing **ΔE/ΔN order** into atan2 (azimuth uses atan2(ΔE, ΔN)). · Reading **r** when you wanted **θ** (or vice versa) after R▸P — press `[x⇄y]`. · Forgetting the **quadrant constant** so a SW azimuth reads as NE. · `PT = PC + L` (arc), **not** PC + 2T. · **÷27** for cubic yards, **÷43,560** for acres — don't swap. · Leaving an item **blank** (always guess).

questions.question_text: Which calculator operation most directly gives the distance AND azimuth between two known coordinates?
questions.explanation: Feeding ΔE and ΔN into R▸P returns the distance (r) and direction (θ) in one step — exactly the inverse. P▸R does the reverse (forward) computation; the other modes are unrelated.

questions.question_text: Which calculator operation most directly gives the coordinates of a new point from a known azimuth and distance?
questions.explanation: P▸R turns (distance, azimuth) into rectangular components (ΔE, ΔN), which you add to the start point. This is the forward computation ΔN = D·cos(Az), ΔE = D·sin(Az) in one operation.

questions.question_text: Before doing any trigonometric (curve, inverse, traverse) computation on an approved calculator, you should verify the calculator is in:
questions.explanation: Survey angles are in degrees, so the calculator must be in DEG. A calculator left in RAD or GRAD gives wildly wrong trig results with no error message — the classic silent blunder.

questions.question_text: Which calculators are on the NCEES approved list for 2026 exams?
options: Casio fx-115/fx-991, HP 33s/35s, and TI-30X/TI-36X models
TI-84 Plus and Casio fx-9750 graphing models
questions.correct_answer: Casio fx-115/fx-991, HP 33s/35s, and TI-30X/TI-36X models
questions.explanation: The 2026 list permits all Casio fx-115 and fx-991 models, only the HP 33s and HP 35s, and all TI-30X and TI-36X models. No programmable, graphing, or CAS calculators are allowed.

questions.question_text: Under the convention used for COGO on a scientific calculator, which axis represents ΔN (latitude)?
questions.explanation: By convention x = ΔE (Easting/departure) and y = ΔN (Northing/latitude), with the azimuth measured from north. R▸P and P▸R map between (ΔE, ΔN) and (distance, azimuth).

questions.question_text: To convert the bearing S 40°00′ W to an azimuth you would compute:
questions.explanation: A southwest bearing converts by Az = 180° + β. So S 40°00′ W → 180° + 40° = 220°00′ azimuth.

questions.question_text: The back azimuth of a line whose forward azimuth is 312°15′ is:
questions.explanation: Back azimuth = (Az + 180°) mod 360° = 312°15′ − 180° = 132°15′ (subtract 180° when Az > 180°).

questions.question_text: When entering an inverse on the calculator, the robust one-line azimuth formula uses which argument order for atan2?
options: atan2(ΔE, ΔN) — easting first, northing second
atan2(ΔN, ΔE) — northing first, easting second
questions.correct_answer: atan2(ΔE, ΔN) — easting first, northing second
questions.explanation: Azimuth is measured clockwise from north (the ΔN axis), so the azimuth is atan2(ΔE, ΔN). Reversing the order gives an angle measured from east instead and produces wrong azimuths.

questions.question_text: On a TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991, after an R▸P conversion you see the distance but need the angle. You should:
options: press the [x⇄y] (swap) key to read θ
questions.correct_answer: press the [x⇄y] (swap) key to read θ
questions.explanation: R▸P returns both r and θ; the swap key (or reading the second output field) displays the angle without re-entering the coordinates.

questions.question_text: Adding the DMS angles 22°45′30″ and 17°20′40″ gives:
questions.explanation: 30″ + 40″ = 70″ = 1′10″; 45′ + 20′ + 1′ = 66′ = 1°06′; 22° + 17° + 1° = 40°. Result 40°06′10″. The calculator does this directly in DMS mode.

questions.question_text: Why is storing a running coordinate total with STO/RCL preferred over re-typing it on each traverse leg?
options: It avoids re-keying errors (blunders) and saves time
It changes the angle mode automatically
It is required by NCEES rules
It increases the calculator's display precision
questions.correct_answer: It avoids re-keying errors (blunders) and saves time
questions.explanation: Every re-typed number is a chance to introduce a blunder. Storing running Northing/Easting totals (and reused constants) in memory both prevents transcription errors and speeds up leg-by-leg traversing.

questions.question_text: On a single vertical photo problem you compute f/(H−h) = 1×10⁻⁴ and need the scale denominator. The fastest keystroke is:
options: press the reciprocal x⁻¹ key to read 10,000
questions.correct_answer: press the reciprocal x⁻¹ key to read 10,000
questions.explanation: Scale is a ratio; pressing x⁻¹ turns 1×10⁻⁴ into 10,000, i.e., a scale of 1:10,000. The reciprocal key is also handy for relative-precision 1:N ratios.

questions.question_text: Which statement about programmable/graphing calculators on the FS exam is correct?
options: They are prohibited; only models on the NCEES approved list may be used
Any calculator is allowed if its memory is cleared
Graphing calculators are allowed if they are not used for graphing
Programmable calculators are allowed if the programs are survey-related
questions.correct_answer: They are prohibited; only models on the NCEES approved list may be used
questions.explanation: NCEES bans programmable, graphing, and CAS calculators outright. Only models on the current approved list (Casio fx-115/991, HP 33s/35s, TI-30X/36X) are permitted, and memory should be cleared.

questions.question_text: Approximately how much time per question does the FS allow (110 questions in 5 h 20 min)?
questions.explanation: 320 minutes ÷ 110 questions ≈ 2.9 minutes each. Banking quick boundary/ethics/definition points buys time for multi-step COGO problems.

questions.question_text: The best test-taking strategy when you reach a long multi-step COGO problem early in the exam is to:
options: flag it, skip ahead to bank quick points, and return to it later
spend as long as needed before moving on
skip it permanently and leave it blank
questions.correct_answer: flag it, skip ahead to bank quick points, and return to it later
questions.explanation: Flag-and-return preserves momentum: answer the fast questions first, then come back to the time-consuming problem with the time you banked. Never leave it blank — there is no penalty for guessing.

questions.question_text: Because the FS has no penalty for wrong answers, you should:
options: answer every question, guessing if necessary, before time expires
leave hard questions blank to avoid lowering your score
answer only the questions you are certain about
randomly fill all answers at the start
questions.correct_answer: answer every question, guessing if necessary, before time expires
questions.explanation: With no penalty for incorrect answers, a blank can only score zero while a guess has a chance. Always answer every question; eliminate wrong options first to improve guess odds.

questions.question_text: The electronic NCEES FS Reference Handbook is most efficiently used during the exam by:
options: knowing in advance where each formula lives so you confirm rather than hunt
reading it cover-to-cover during the exam
ignoring it and working from memory only
searching it for every single question
questions.correct_answer: knowing in advance where each formula lives so you confirm rather than hunt
questions.explanation: The handbook is searchable but large. Practicing with the actual PDF beforehand so you know where the curve, area, inverse, and statistics formulas sit lets you confirm a formula in seconds instead of burning time hunting.

questions.question_text: A surveyor needs to area a simple 60 ft × 120 ft rectangle. The most efficient calculator approach is to:
options: run a full coordinate (shoelace) routine on all four corners
enter all corners into a stored AREA program
questions.explanation: Part-D calculation discipline: use the simplest method. A rectangle's area is just side × side — running a full traverse/area routine wastes time and invites entry errors.

questions.question_text: On the area-by-coordinates (shoelace) method, which variables are conventionally taken as X and Y?
questions.explanation: In the coordinate (shoelace) area method, X is the Easting and Y is the Northing; you sum the cross-products Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ, halve, and take the absolute value.

questions.question_text: Which is the most reliable way to determine the correct quadrant of an inverse azimuth on the calculator?
options: use atan2(ΔE, ΔN), or read the signs of ΔN and ΔE
always use the acute angle from atan
questions.correct_answer: use atan2(ΔE, ΔN), or read the signs of ΔN and ΔE
questions.explanation: atan2 returns the angle in the correct quadrant automatically. Equivalently, the signs of ΔN and ΔE place the line in NE/SE/SW/NW, to which you add the handbook quadrant constant. A bare atan loses the quadrant.

questions.question_text: True or False: For the curve-length quickie, when the degree of curve D is given, L = (I/D)·100 lets you avoid using π entirely.
questions.explanation: True. With D known, L = (I/D)·100 gives the arc length directly in feet without π or radians — a faster keystroke chain than L = R·I·(π/180).

questions.question_text: True or False: You may use a calculator with stored COGO programs (e.g., an HP traverse routine) on the FS exam as long as it is an HP 35s.
questions.explanation: False. The HP 35s is on the approved list, but NCEES expects calculator memory to be cleared and prohibits using stored programs/notes during the exam. The HP program books are study/field references, not exam tools.

questions.question_text: Convert 132°15′00″ to decimal degrees.
Express 1°30′45″ as a total number of seconds.

problem_templates.name: DMS to total seconds (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE — Express the angle {{D:f0}}°{{M:f0}}′{{S:f0}}″ as a TOTAL number of seconds.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Total seconds = D·3600 + M·60 + S = {{D:f0}}·3600 + {{M:f0}}·60 + {{S:f0}} = {{_answer}} seconds. A fast mental/keystroke drill that underlies DMS arithmetic.

problem_templates.name: DMS + DMS angle sum (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE — Add the two angles {{D1:f0}}°{{M1:f0}}′{{S1:f0}}″ and {{D2:f0}}°{{M2:f0}}′{{S2:f0}}″ and report the SUM in DECIMAL degrees.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Enter both angles in DMS, press +, and the calculator returns the sum; here in decimal it is ({{D1:f0}}+{{M1:f0}}/60+{{S1:f0}}/3600) + ({{D2:f0}}+{{M2:f0}}/60+{{S2:f0}}/3600) = {{_answer}}°. Toggle ▸DMS for the field-book form.

problem_templates.name: Inverse distance via R▸P (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE (R▸P) — Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}) and point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Using rectangular-to-polar, compute the horizontal DISTANCE A→B (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Compute ΔN = {{dN:f2}} and ΔE = {{dE:f2}}, feed them to R▸P, and read the distance r = hypot(ΔN, ΔE) = {{_answer}} ft. One R▸P replaces the separate √ and atan.

problem_templates.name: Inverse azimuth via R▸P (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE (R▸P) — Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}) and point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Using rectangular-to-polar, compute the AZIMUTH A→B in decimal degrees (0–360°).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Azimuth is measured from north: Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) normalized to 0–360°. With ΔE = {{dE:f2}}, ΔN = {{dN:f2}}, Az = {{_answer}}°. On the calculator, R▸P returns this θ directly — read it with the swap key.

problem_templates.name: Forward coordinate via P▸R (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE (P▸R) — From point P (N {{nP:f2}}, E {{eP:f2}}) go azimuth {{az:f2}}° a distance of {{dist:f2}} ft. Using polar-to-rectangular, compute the NORTHING of the new point (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Feed distance {{dist:f2}} and azimuth {{az:f2}}° to P▸R; the latitude ΔN = D·cos(Az) = {{dN:f2}} ft. New Northing = {{nP:f2}} + {{dN:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Bearing to azimuth (SW quadrant drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE — A line has the bearing S {{beta:f2}}° W. Compute its AZIMUTH (decimal degrees, 0–360°).
problem_templates.explanation_template: A southwest bearing converts by Az = 180° + β = 180 + {{beta:f2}} = {{_answer}}°. (NE: β; SE: 180−β; SW: 180+β; NW: 360−β.)

problem_templates.name: Area by coordinates (triangle, shoelace drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE — A triangular parcel has corners, in order, A (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}), B (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}), and C (N {{nC:f2}}, E {{eC:f2}}). Using the coordinate (shoelace) method, compute the area in SQUARE FEET.

solution_steps_template.title: Sum of cross-products, halved, absolute value

problem_templates.explanation_template: Apply the shoelace sum with X = Easting, Y = Northing around A→B→C, halve the absolute value: A = ½|Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)| = {{_answer}} ft². Chain the three cross-products with memory/SUM to avoid re-keying.

problem_templates.name: Horizontal-curve quickie — tangent T (calculator drill)
problem_templates.question_template: CALCULATOR CHALLENGE — A circular curve has R = {{R:f2}} ft and deflection angle I = {{I:f2}}°. In DEGREE mode, compute the tangent distance T (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: In DEGREE mode, type I/2 straight into tan: T = R·tan(I/2) = {{R:f2}}·tan({{I:f2}}°/2) = {{_answer}} ft.

mock_questions.question_text: Which single calculator operation most directly returns both the distance and the azimuth between two coordinates?
mock_questions.explanation: R▸P takes (ΔE, ΔN) and returns (distance r, direction θ) in one step — the inverse computation. P▸R does the reverse.

mock_questions.question_text: To find the coordinates of a new point from a known azimuth and distance, the calculator operation is:
mock_questions.explanation: P▸R converts (distance, azimuth) to rectangular ΔE and ΔN, which add to the start coordinates — the forward computation ΔN = D·cos(Az), ΔE = D·sin(Az).

mock_questions.question_text: Adding 35°50′40″ and 12°15′30″ in DMS mode gives:
Converting the azimuth 215°00′ to a bearing gives:
mock_questions.explanation: 215° is in the SW quadrant (180–270). Bearing = S (Az − 180°) W = S (215 − 180)° W = S 35°00′ W.

mock_questions.question_text: The single most important calculator setting to verify before any survey trig is:
mock_questions.explanation: Survey angles are in degrees; a calculator in RAD or GRAD produces wildly wrong trig with no warning. Always confirm DEG before curve, inverse, or traverse work.

mock_questions.question_text: Computing area from corner coordinates by the shoelace method, the cross-product to accumulate for each edge i is:
mock_questions.explanation: Area = ½·|Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)| with X = Easting, Y = Northing. Accumulate each edge's cross-product, halve, take the absolute value.

mock_questions.question_text: A circular curve has R = 600 ft and I = 30°. Its tangent distance T = R·tan(I/2) is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: T = 600·tan(15°) = 600·0.26795 = 160.77 ft. Type I/2 straight into tan in DEGREE mode.

mock_questions.question_text: On the FS exam (110 questions in 5 h 20 min), the recommended pacing strategy is to:
options: answer easy questions first, flag-and-skip hard ones, and return later
solve questions strictly in order regardless of difficulty
spend equal fixed time on every question
do all computational questions first, in full, before any others
mock_questions.correct_answer: answer easy questions first, flag-and-skip hard ones, and return later
mock_questions.explanation: At ~2.9 min/question, banking the quick boundary/ethics/definition points and flagging long COGO problems preserves momentum and protects time for multi-step items.

mock_questions.question_text: Which calculator is NOT permitted on the FS exam under the 2026 approved list?
mock_questions.explanation: Graphing (and programmable/CAS) calculators like the TI-84 are prohibited. The fx-991EX, TI-36X Pro, and HP 35s are all on the approved list.

mock_questions.question_text: After an R▸P conversion shows the distance, the angle (azimuth) is read by:
options: pressing the [x⇄y] swap key (or reading the θ field)
mock_questions.correct_answer: pressing the [x⇄y] swap key (or reading the θ field)
mock_questions.explanation: R▸P outputs both r and θ; the swap key (or the second result field) shows the angle without re-keying the inputs.

mock_questions.question_text: Because the FS imposes no penalty for wrong answers, the correct exam behavior is to:
options: answer every question, guessing among remaining choices if unsure
answer only questions you are fully confident about
guess all answers at the start to save time
mock_questions.correct_answer: answer every question, guessing among remaining choices if unsure
mock_questions.explanation: A blank scores zero; a guess (especially after eliminating wrong options) has a chance. Never leave a question unanswered.

mock_questions.question_text: The most efficient way to use the searchable electronic FS Reference Handbook during the exam is to:
options: know in advance where each formula section lives so you confirm, not hunt
read it fully during the exam
memorize it and never open it
search it from scratch for every question
mock_questions.correct_answer: know in advance where each formula section lives so you confirm, not hunt
mock_questions.explanation: The handbook is large but searchable; practicing with the real PDF so you know where curves, area, inverse, and statistics formulas sit turns a confirmation into seconds rather than a time-consuming hunt.

mock_questions.question_text: The back azimuth of a line with forward azimuth 78°30′ is:
To area a simple rectangular lot quickly, the best practice is to:
options: multiply the two side lengths directly rather than run a full COGO/area routine
always run the stored AREA program
convert each corner with R▸P first
compute the diagonal and use Heron's formula
mock_questions.correct_answer: multiply the two side lengths directly rather than run a full COGO/area routine
mock_questions.explanation: Calculation discipline: use the simplest method. A rectangle is side × side; running a full coordinate routine wastes time and invites entry errors.
